## Background

In today’s class we will be employing all the R techniques for data analysis that we have learned thus far - including the machine learning methods of clustering and PCA - to analyze real breast cancer biopsy data.

## Data Import

The data is in CSV format:

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
wiscancer <- read.csv("WisconsinCancer.csv", row.names=1)
```
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
head(wiscancer, 3)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```{.r .cell-code}
length( grep ( "_mean" , colnames( wiscancer ) ) )
```

```
[1] 10
```


:::
:::


We need to remove the `diagnosis` column before we do any further analysis of this dataset - we don't want to pass this to PCA etc. We will save it as a separate vector that we can use later to compare our findings to those of experts. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
diagnosis <- wiscancer [,1] #saves the diagnosis
wisc <- wiscancer[,-1] #dataframe omitting the first column 
```
:::


## Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

The main function in base R is called `prcomp()` we will use the optional argument `scale=T` here as the data columns/features/dimensions are on very different scales in the original data set 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
wisc.pr <- prcomp(wisc, scale=T)
```
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(wisc.pr$x) +
  aes(PC1, PC2, col=diagnosis) +
  geom_point()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](class08_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-8-1.png){width=672}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
summary(wisc.pr)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Importance of components:
                          PC1    PC2     PC3     PC4     PC5     PC6     PC7

```


:::
:::

> Q4. From your results, what proportion of the original variance is captured by the first principal component (PC1)?

0.4427

> Q5. How many principal components (PCs) are required to describe at least 70% of the original variance in the data?

Three

> Q6. How many principal components (PCs) are required to describe at least 90% of the original variance in the data?

Seven
# Repeat for components 1 and 3

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(wisc.pr$x) +
  aes(PC1, PC3, col=diagnosis) +
  geom_point()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](class08_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-9-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


# Calculate variance of each component


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pr.var <- wisc.pr$sdev^2
head(pr.var)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 13.281608  5.691355  2.817949  1.980640  1.648731  1.207357
```


:::
:::

# Variance explained by each principal component: pve


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pve <- pr.var / sum(pr.var)
```
:::


# Plot variance explained for each principal component


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(c(1,pve), xlab = "Principal Component", 
     ylab = "Proportion of Variance Explained", 
     ylim = c(0, 1), type = "o")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](class08_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-12-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::




# Alternative scree plot of the same data, note data driven y-axis


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
barplot(pve, ylab = "Percent of Variance Explained",
     names.arg=paste0("PC",1:length(pve)), las=2, axes = FALSE)
axis(2, at=pve, labels=round(pve,2)*100 )
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](class08_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-13-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## ggplot based graph

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(factoextra)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Welcome! Want to learn more? See two factoextra-related books at https://goo.gl/ve3WBa
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
fviz_eig(wisc.pr, addlabels = TRUE)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning in geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = barfill, color = barcolor, :
Ignoring empty aesthetic: `width`.
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](class08_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-14-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

> Q9. For the first principal component, what is the component of the loading vector (i.e. wisc.pr$rotation[,1]) for the feature concave.points_mean? This tells us how much this original feature contributes to the first PC. Are there any features with larger contributions than this one?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pc1 <- wisc.pr$rotation [, "PC1"]
wisc.pr$rotation["concave.points_mean",1]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] -0.2608538
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
min(pc1)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] -0.2608538
```


:::
:::


# Scale the wisc.data data using the "scale()" function

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
wisc.data <- wisc
data.scaled <- scale(wisc.data)

data.dist <- dist(data.scaled)

wisc.hclust <- hclust(data.dist)

plot(wisc.hclust)
abline(h=19.3, col="red", lty=2)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](class08_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-16-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::





## Combining methods

The idea here is that Ic an take my new variables (the scores on the PCs which are in `wisc.pr$x`) that are better descriptors of the data-set than the original features (ie the 30 columns in `wisc.data`) and use these as a basis for clustering. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pc.dist <- dist(wisc.pr$x[, 1:3])
wisc.pr.hclust <- hclust(pc.dist, method = "ward.D2")
plot(wisc.pr.hclust)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](class08_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-17-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
grps <- cutree(wisc.pr.hclust, k=2)
table(grps)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
grps
  1   2 
203 366 
```


:::
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
wisc.hclust.clusters <- cutree(wisc.hclust, k=4)
table(wisc.hclust.clusters, diagnosis)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                    diagnosis
wisc.hclust.clusters   B   M
                   1  12 165
                   2   2   5
                   3 343  40
                   4   0   2
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(wisc.pr$x) +
  aes(PC1, PC2) +
  geom_point(col=grps)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](class08_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-19-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


I can now run `table()` with both my clustering `grps` and the expert `diagnosis`


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
table(grps, diagnosis)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
    diagnosis
grps   B   M
   1  24 179
   2 333  33
```


:::
:::


Our cluster "1" has 179 "M" Diagnosis
Our cluster "2" has 333 "B" Diagnosis

179 TP
24 FP

333 TN
33 FN

Sensitivity: TP / (TP+FN)

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
179 / (179+33)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 0.8443396
```


:::
:::


Specificity: TN / (TN+FP)

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
333 / (333+24)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 0.9327731
```


:::
:::


## Prediction

We can use our PCA model for prediction of 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
url <- "https://tinyurl.com/new-samples-CSV"
new <- read.csv(url)
npc <- predict(wisc.pr, newdata=new)
npc
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
           PC1       PC2        PC3        PC4       PC5        PC6        PC7

[2,] -4.754928 -3.009033 -0.1660946 -0.6052952 -1.140698 -1.2189945  0.8193031
            PC8       PC9       PC10      PC11      PC12      PC13     PC14

[2,] -0.3307423 0.5281896 -0.4855301 0.7173233 -1.185917 0.5893856 0.303029
          PC15       PC16        PC17        PC18        PC19       PC20
[1,] 0.3216974 -0.1743616 -0.07875393 -0.11207028 -0.08802955 -0.2495216
[2,] 0.1299153  0.1448061 -0.40509706  0.06565549  0.25591230 -0.4289500
           PC21       PC22       PC23       PC24        PC25         PC26
[1,]  0.1228233 0.09358453 0.08347651  0.1223396  0.02124121  0.078884581
[2,] -0.1224776 0.01732146 0.06316631 -0.2338618 -0.20755948 -0.009833238
             PC27        PC28         PC29         PC30
[1,]  0.220199544 -0.02946023 -0.015620933  0.005269029
[2,] -0.001134152  0.09638361  0.002795349 -0.019015820
```


:::
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plot(wisc.pr$x[,1:2], col=grps)
points(npc[,1], npc[,2], col="blue", pch=16, cex=3)
text(npc[,1], npc[,2], c(1,2), col="white")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](class08_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-24-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::

> Q16 Follow up with patient 1 because it has signs that match up with malignant.
